step_by_step_thinking: \boxed{B} No — While erenumab is approved for preventing chronic migraines via targeting calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP), there is no mention in any of the retrieved articles of erenumab's use or efficacy in treating trigeminal neuralgia. Standard first-line treatments for trigeminal neuralgia involve antiseizure medications like carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine, which act on voltage-gated sodium channels. Eruminab’s mechanism of action does not align with established pathways for trigeminal neuralgia pathophysiology, and no studies cited support its application in this disorder. Therefore, based solely on the given information, erenumab is not effective for trigeminal neuralgia.
raw_response:
\boxed{B} No — While erenumab is approved for preventing chronic migraines via targeting calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP), there is no mention in any of the retrieved articles of erenumab's use or efficacy in treating trigeminal neuralgia. Standard first-line treatments for trigeminal neuralgia involve antiseizure medications like carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine, which act on voltage-gated sodium channels. Eruminab’s mechanism of action does not align with established pathways for trigeminal neuralgia pathophysiology, and no studies cited support its application in this disorder. Therefore, based solely on the given information, erenumab is not effective for trigeminal neuralgia.
\boxed{B}
